summarization.a: This panel shows the N 1s XPS spectrum of Ti–Si–N. It contains Ti–N and Si–N bonding components.
summarization.b: This panel shows the Si 2p XPS spectrum of Ti–Si–N. The peak matches Si–N bonding.

data_extraction.a:
| Peak assignment | Curve style | Binding energy (eV) | Relative intensity |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Ti–N bonding    | Dash-dot    | ~397.0              | High              |
| Si–N bonding    | Dashed      | ~398.5              | Medium            |
| Total N 1s      | Solid       | ~397.2 (envelope)   | Total             |
data_extraction.b:
| Peak assignment | Curve style | Binding energy (eV) | Chemical state |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Si₃N₄-like      | Solid       | ~102.0              | Si–N bonding  |

a.question: What analysis step is implied by the presence of multiple curves under the N 1s peak?
Why does the N 1s spectrum require more than one fitted component?
a.answer: Nitrogen bonds to both titanium and silicon in the film. Each bonding environment shifts the binding energy slightly. Multiple components are therefore required to represent the spectrum.

a.question: Why is mixed Ti–N and Si–N bonding beneficial for barrier materials?
a.answer: Mixed bonding increases structural disorder. This disrupts diffusion pathways. It improves barrier performance.

b.question: Which chemical bonding state is indicated by the Si 2p peak?
b.answer: Si–N bonding consistent with a Si₃N₄-like chemical environment.
